title: Greek Salad

overview.description: A classic Greek village salad of ripe tomatoes, cucumber, green pepper, red onion, olives and feta, dressed with olive oil and oregano.

ingredients: 600g ripe tomatoes, cut into large chunks
1 large cucumber, cut into thick half-moons
1 green pepper, deseeded and sliced into rings
½ tsp salt, or to taste
method: Place the tomatoes in a large serving bowl.
Add the cucumber, green pepper and red onion.
Season lightly with salt and toss gently.
Drizzle over the olive oil and red wine vinegar if using.
Sprinkle over the oregano and add black pepper.
Place the feta on top in one large piece or in large slabs rather than crumbling it finely.
Drizzle the feta with a little extra olive oil.
Finish with another pinch of oregano.
Serve immediately or allow to stand for 10–15 minutes before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Traditional Greek village salad is generally made without lettuce. The combination of ripe tomatoes, cucumber, peppers, olives, onion and feta is known as horiatiki, meaning village-style.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to build a simple salad around high-quality ingredients and how salt draws flavour and moisture from vegetables to create a natural dressing.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A complete, fresh and simple meal that introduces the salad section with a classic recipe that can also be easily scaled down as a side dish.
chapman-tips: Use the ripest, most flavourful tomatoes available.
Don't crumble the feta too finely; large pieces are more traditional.
Kalamata olives with stones generally have better flavour and texture.
Salt the vegetables lightly rather than heavily salting the dressing.
Greek oregano has a particularly strong aroma and is worth using if available.
The salad should be dressed shortly before serving.
Serve with good bread to soak up the tomato and olive oil juices.
For a more substantial meal, add white beans or serve with grilled bread.
notes.batch-cooking: Not suitable for freezing. Best prepared fresh.
notes.leftovers: Best eaten the same day. Leftover dressed vegetables can be used in a wrap or served with toasted bread.
